meaning: in lafe ላፈ, 'to this side, in the direction of, that way'; walafe ዋላፈ 'and beyond, and above'; lafe...walafe 'this way...and that way, on this side...and on that side, here...and there'; lafe walafe (Li) 'on either side'; ǝmlafe-hi...wǝǝmlafe-hi 'on every side, on one side...and on the other side'; balafe...balafe 'on this side...(and) on that side'; ǝnta lafe...wǝǝnta lafe 'within and without, hither and thither'; ǝmkahā...wǝǝmlafe 'on this side...and on that side'
origin_explanation: Dillmann 65 records lafe as the lexeme whereas lafe is more likely to be analyzed as la-fe, with agglutinated la (so also Praetorius 1879:143; Brockelmann 1908:333). SEMITIC: Heb. pǝ'ā(h) 'side, edge', Aram. pā(y)-tā, Ug. pit 'corner', Akk. pāṭu 'edge', pūṭu 'front'; Ar. fi'a 'troop, band' is given by Noeldeke 1910:152 an original meaning 'Angriffsfront'. Rundgren 1955:70 connects fe with Heb. pō 'here'. For this biradical root, see Noeldeke 1910:151. ETHIOPIC: Amharic fit 'front'. For lafaya ላፋያ, see below.

meaning: 'woof, spun yarn, opening of a tunic'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. fǝ'mi 'the thread that remains in the shuttle for weaving'. Dillmann 1374 connects the meanings of fǝ'm 'morsel' and fǝ'm 'woof', and for the relation between the meanings he compares Ar. luḥma 'woof' and 'bait'.

meaning: 'put a morsel of food in another's mouth, feed, give bread to a beggar'; tafǝ'ma (T) tafǝ'ma 'put food into one's mouth, put a morsel into the mouth, feed on, beg for bread'; tafā'ama (K*) 'put food in one another's mouths'; ǝstafā'ama 'beg for a morsel, beg'; ǝstafā'ama (K*), caus. of tafā'ama; fā'm (pl. ǝfǝ'mī, ǝf'amī, fǝ'māt, ǝf'ām) 'morsel, small bread divided among the poor'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. fa'ama 'fill its mouth with herbage (camel)'; and perhaps Heb. pīmā 'fat' < *pǝ'imā. Von Soden 862 compares Heb. pīmā with Akk. piyām/nu, "etwa 'stark'" (so also Koehler 759). The identification of G. f'm with Ar. fam 'mouth' (a secondary form of fū), suggested by Dillmann 1374, is less likely. ETHIOPIC: Gaf (tā)famā 'take a mouthful'; Amh. fämma (from f'm?), as against f'm (Gafat), dābtǝra-argot for ḥālla 'eat'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. fāqā, same.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. fǝg 'dung', (a)fāgā 'to manure with dung'. Gur. fīg 'hay left after the cows have eaten'; possibly related to fāgaga I.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. fāqā, same.
for -lawa, see fǝrǝlw, in ǝsfarlawa. The form is strange.

ETHIOPIC: Amh. fu alā 'hiss (snake), snort (ox, from anger)', fīw alā 'whistle'; is possibly connected with G. ǝf'ot 'viper'. There is also the possibility that KWK simply transcribed Heb. pǝ'ā (p'y) 'groan'.

meaning: 'increase, augment, exceed, excel, become numerous, be copious, be abundant, multiply, surpass, be superior'; ǝfaddada 'multiply (tr.), augment, surpass, make exceed, give in abundance, do in abundance, do more'; tafaddada, passive; faddad 'excellent, abundant, excessive, extreme'; faddād 'excellence, superiority, advantage, cess; superfluous'; faddādāt (pl. of faddād) 'appendages'; faddād-a 'abundantly, exceedingly, extraordinarily, in particular, greatly, above all, more in a high degree'; (ǝfǝfo faddāda [Lt] 'how much more!'); faddāda-ssa 'chiefly, especially, particularly, above all'; faddād 'superiority, excellence, pre-eminence'; (faddād-a 'exceedingly, especially, very'); faddāde 'abundance, superiority, advantage, excellence'; faddādnā 'excellence, superiority'; mafaddād 'major part, the greater part'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: SAr. fdfd-t-m 'reichlich tragend' (Müller 1983:279); perhaps also related to Ar. fāda (fyd) 'be useful, be beneficial'. ETHIOPIC: Te. fādfāda 'have many children', fādfud 'large, plentiful', Tna. fādfāda 'exceed, be plentiful', Amh. fādfādā; also in Cushitic: Som. faḍ 'be in abundance'; related to Som. Sa. bad 'abundance'.

meaning: 'be numerous, be abundant, be in excess, exceed the limits, be left over'
origin_explanation: from Ar. faḍala 'be left over, excel'; also in Te. (tā)faddāla 'excel', faḍl 'excellent, great', Amh. faḍǝl 'large, stocky person'.
is either related to bdl (Heb. bdl, hif'il 'separate') or is to be connected with fidal 'letter' (see below), that is, 'separated, set apart'. See also fdl I, ǝsfadlawā.
to be connected with fadala II 'separate'. Or could the root be sfd with the enigmatic ending -lawa, as in fǝrǝlw, ǝsfǝrǝlawa?

meaning: 'letter of the Ethiopian syllabary, alphabet, syllabary'; fadala (T.MA) 'write, master the alphabet' (denominative)
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. fīdel 'letter', Tna.Amh. fīdāl, Amh. fāddālā 'write'; perhaps derived from fadala 'separate', that is, something that is separated or set apart, that forms a unit.

meaning: 'puncture, pierce, cut a vein in the forehead in order to bleed or cup a person'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. fāddāsa 'make an incision (e.g. on the forehead to cure a headache)', Tna.Amh. fāddāsa; Amh. also fānāddāsa (with augmented n) 'split'.

meaning: 'recompense, restore, remunerate, requite, reward, substitute, pay back, repay, pay a penalty, pay a debt, owe a debt'; (fadaya baqala 'take revenge'); (zayǝfaddi 'debtor'); ǝfdaya, causative; tafadya, passive; also 'exact payment, demand payment, receive something in compensation, obtain indemnity, receive payment, receive a due award, suffer vengeance, take revenge, avenge, receive punishment, bear punishment'; fadāy, fadāyi, act. part. of fadaya; also 'answerable'; faddā 'retribution, remuneration, repayment, recompense, reward, compensation, ransom, punishment, revenge'; faddā 'for' (for the passage of meaning, see baqala, bezā); fadǝy 'payment, repayment, retribution'; fadyat (n.) 'paying, rewarding, compensating'; mafde 'money, reward, payment'; mafdāy 'that with which a debt was paid'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. fadā (fdy) 'buy off, ransom', SAr. fdy 'redeem (a debt), pay (money)', Soq. fede 'ransom'.

ETHIOPIC: Tna. fädäyä 'pay, repay, requite'. Te. fäda 'finish, fulfill', Har. fäqa 'exterminate' (Cerulli 1936:415), Gur. fäqä 'accomplish, finish, fulfill'. The final z of Te. fäza 'ransom' in relation to the final d of G. fäddä is explained by Brockelmann 1927:21 as 'Angleichung' to bezä (see above). However, the alternance d:z in Tigre is a frequent occurrence.

meaning: 'obstruct, close up, shut, stop up, make speechless, silence, (TS.KG) smear (on), (Y) put on a bit, crush, muzzle'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Šh fozum 'stop someone's mouth'. ETHIOPIC: Gur. fäṭämä 'close the eyes or the mouth, block the road'; related to faṣṣama (see below).

meaning: 'be feverish, be sick with fever, shiver, (K*.TS) have diarrhea, (K*.Y.M) be angry' (unless fanaḍaḍa, the translation of faṣana, is a denominative of nədad 'fever'; tänaddädädä would then mean 'be feverish').
'have a bad odor, exhale a bad odor, be fetid; fəgg 'foul, fetid, rank, bad odor'; fagg (Gr.388) 'dung, stench'; fäggag (K*) 'stench, bad odor''.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. fāğa (fwğ) 'exhale an odor'. Baumgartner 860 compares, with reservations, Ar. fīğ 'unripe'. Heb. *pag 'aram, paggā. ETHIOPIC: Te. fäggägä 'putrefy, rot', Tna. fäggägä 'disgust, nauseate', Amh. fäggägä 'have a foul odor'; passed into Cushitic: Bil. fägäg 'rot'. See also fərg.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. fäggägä 'light up (sky), smile'; passed into Cushitic: Qem. fägäg 'smile'.

meaning: 'scratch the ground, be intent (on a task), strive, work hard, be exhausted'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. fağara 'cleave, cut, break open', PbH. pigger 'break up, destroy', Aram. paggar. ETHIOPIC: Tna. fägärä 'make a hole in a wall to enter the house with the intention of robbing', Amh. fäggärä, foggärä 'paw the ground, dig the dirt', Te. fagra 'go out' comes close to the meaning of Ar. fağara (see above).

meaning: 'live a life of pleasure, find pleasure in, live in luxury, live sumptuously, enjoy oneself, be given to delight'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. fägəgə 'comfort', Tna. tafgəgət 'reveling'.
SEMITIC: PbH. pāhaq 'yawn'; related to Ar. fāqa 'have hiccups, be afflicted with panting'. See also fahaqa II. ETHIOPIC: Amh. (a)faqä 'have hiccups'.

meaning: 'put to flight, chase away, separate, flee'.
origin_explanation: perhaps connected with fahaqa I 'breathe out' which could have come from the meaning 'chase away' > 'chase out, 'send out' > 'breathe out'.

meaning: 'be lascivious, be impudent, (T.Y.M) be easily angered, seethe with anger, be sexually aroused (animal), get drunk'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. fahl 'stallion (D 1347), putting of a camel among she-camels', Mh. fahl 'penis', Syr. pəhlā 'yearning for coition', pāhlā 'testicle', pīhlā 'male organ', Ug. phl 'ass', Akk. puḫālu 'lion used for covering'.

SEMITIC: Ar. faḥm 'charcoal', Soq. fḥam, Heb. peḥam, Syr. paḥmā, Ug.Ph. phm, Akk. pēntu (for pēm tu). ETHIOPIC: Te. fäḥam 'charcoal', Tna. fəḥmi, fəḥam, Amh. fəm, Gur. fem, Har. fəḥäma 'be hot'; passed into Cushitic: Or. fimi 'charcoal, embers', Qab. fəma, Kham. feḥmā.

meaning: 'scrape, erase, scratch smooth (parchment), tan, engrave, carve, polish, wipe off, wipe away, rub off, scrub, clean, (T) separate, chase away'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 1348 connects it with Ar. maḥaq 'efface, strike out' (with alternance of labials); in this case, cp. also Heb. māḥaq 'skin' (v), PbH. māḥaq 'wipe off'. ETHIOPIC: Te. fäḥaqa 'gnaw, exterminate', Tna. fäḥaqä 'a 'scrape a bone' (also fälḥaqä 'a 'remove the fiber from a bark', with augmented ḥ), Arg. fäqaqä 'tan', Har. fəḥäqa 'tan, scrub', Amh. faqä, faqafäqä (reduplicated faqä), Gur. faqä; also in Cushitic: Aw. faki, Som. fiq 'scrape', Kham. faq.

SEMITIC: Ar. fā'ara 'dig, notch cut' (Dozy). Ar. fa'ara 'dig' mentioned by Amsalu 61 is a denominative from fa'r 'rat, mouse' and would mean 'dig as a rat does'. The root also occurs as hfr, with metathesis, in Ar. ḥafara 'dig', SAr. hfr, Heb. hāpar, Aram. hāpar, Syr. hapar, Md. hpr, Akk. hapāru. ETHIOPIC: Tna. fäḥarä 'dig out', Amh. farä; with a reduplicated root in Amh. färäffara, Gur. färäfära. Tigre has ḥafra 'dig' (with metathesis), as is the case in some Semitic languages; see above.

meaning: '(Y) 'spin (thread), twist, (K*) rub, (K*.H) make red by dyeing''.
origin_explanation: this root can either be divided into fəḥsa I 'spin, twist, rub' (fəḥso 'thread'), and fəḥsa II 'make red by dyeing' (fəḥso 'red color, kind of red flower', fəḥus 'of purple color'), or be represented only as fəḥsa with the basic meaning 'spin, twist, rub', and 'make red by dyeing' would derive from rubbing, mixing, and producing a red color. SEMITIC: for the meaning 'twist, rub', cp. Ar. faḥaṣa 'rub ears of barley to remove the grain', Mh. fḥas 'twist'; related to Ar. faḥaṣa 'scrape the ground (for laying eggs)', PbH. pāḥaš 'smash, beat out of shape, dissolve by rubbing'. ETHIOPIC: Te. fəḥasä 'twist', Tna. fəḥasä, Amh. fəḥəso, fəḥiso 'kind of red flower' (from Geez).
SEMITIC: related to Ar. faḥaṣa 'scrutinize'.
ETHIOPIC: to be identified with G. ḥafata (with metathesis), and ḥanfaṭa I 'scratch with the finger-nail', with metathesis and insertion of n.
SEMITIC: Heb. ḥrp (with metathesis) in neḥrepeṭ la-ʾiš 'engaged (betrothed) to a man' (see Z. Ben-Ḥayyim, Leshonenu 7 [1936].362-366).
SEMITIC: related to Ar. fakiha 'become merry, gay'. ETHIOPIC: Har. fäkāha 'become bright, bloom', Amh. fäkka 'burst open (flower), beam (face), light up (face)'.

SEMITIC: Ar. fakara 'consider, examine mentally'. Brockelmann 1928:568 also compares Syr. pəqar 'bind', and for the relation of meanings he quotes Ar. 'aqala 'bind' and 'aql 'mind, intelligence'. ETHIOPIC: Te. fäkkära 'explain, expound', Tna.Amh. fäkkärä 'interpret, explain'. Amh. also fänäkkärä (with augmented n) 'reveal, open'.

From Ar. faḥara 'brag, boast'; also in Te. fäkkära 'boast of one's exploits'. Tna. fäkkärä, Amh. fäkkärä, Gaf. fäkkärä, Har. (tä)fäkkära, Gur. fäkkärä; in Cushitic: Aw. fekär, Kam. fokkaru. This root is not to be connected with G. fäkkärä I 'interpret' as suggested by Praetorius 1879:7, and Amsalu 62.

phonetics: fikātam I ፊካተም (K.DTW 419, under wəha ዐዋሐ), fikāṭəm ፊካጠም (T.D 1398)
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: perhaps Amh. fokkätä 'nibble (cattle: ground where there is no grass)'. Dillmann 1398 suggests a reading rekāṭəm ረካጠም; T has both fikātam and rekāṭəm. See also fikāṭəm II.

meaning: 'large stream (or river) which passes into a body of water like the Blue Nile or Lake Tana'
origin_explanation: It is strange that this lexeme should have two unconnected meanings. Could it be interpreted by the possibility of the beetle's (called fikāṭəm) being found in such bodies of water?
From Ar. fūl; also Te.Tna.Amh. ful; in the other Semitic languages: Heb. pōl, Aram. pōlā (see Löw 312ff).

meaning: 'gush out, spring forth, bubble up, break forth, burst out as a fountain, (Lt) make gush, dispense, (K*) shell, pierce'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. falla (fll) 'dent, break', Syr. pal (pll) 'sprinkle'. ETHIOPIC: Te. fälfäla 'sprout forth, break through', Tna. fälfäla 'break, make a hole', Amh. fälfäla 'shell (peas, beans), gush out', Har. fälfäla, Gaf. fälfäla 'shell, hatch out, make a hole by scratching'. For 'spring of water', cp. Te. fälfäl, Tna. fälfäl, Amh. fälfäl. The root passed into Cushitic: Bil. fälfäl 'gush out', Qab. fälfällo, falfala. See also falfal.

SEMITIC: Ar. fil 'elephant', PbH. pīl, Arām.-Syr. pīl(a), Akk. pīlu, pīru. The derivation of falfal 'elephant' from falfala 'gush out, break out' suggested by Hommel 376, n. 1 is unlikely. For Ar. fil, Jeffery 230-1 states "it would seem to be of Iranian origin". A. Fischer 1953:101 suggests either a direct borrowing from Persian pīl or through the intermediary of Aram. pīlā. Iranian origin through Aramaic could also apply to Akkadian pīlu.

phonetics: falfal II ፋልፋል (D), falfal ፋልፋል (T.K), falfal ፋልፋል (Lt)
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. filfil, Mh. felfel, PbH. pilpel, Syr. pilpəlā, Md. pilpil, from Sanskrit pippali (see Löw 317). ETHIOPIC: Te. färfär 'black pepper' (with a of liquids), Amh. fälfäl där 'imported pepper stew' passed into Cushitic: Som. filfil, Be. filfil; also barbarre.
ETHIOPIC: Amh.Arg. fälfäl, Gaf. fälfälä, Gur. fälfäl, Har. fifi (from flff); see falfala, above.

meaning: 'flow, (T.M) cause to flow in torrents, dig out, hollow out, vide, split, (Y) hew, prepare, arrange'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. falaga 'split', Heb. pilleg, Aram.-Syr. pəlag, Md.Ug. plg. It is from 'split' that the noun 'river, riverbed' developed. Ar. falag, falīg 'small river, brook', Mh. felēg 'watercourse', M-flg 'exit channel (of dam)'. Heb. peleg 'river', Ug. plg, Akk. palgu 'ditch, channel'. ETHIOPIC: Te. fäläg 'ravine', Tna. fäläg 'riverbed'. Amh. fäläg 'river' (from Geez).

meaning: 'bubble up, boil (intr), both be effervescent, foam, flare up, break out, gush out, (Y) get angry'
origin_explanation: (fəluḥa rəṣuʾ 'swift on a mission'); fälḥat (n.) 'bubbling, boiling, ardor'; mafälḥa (pl. mafäləḥt) 'vessel in which one boils, boiler, a thing that one boils or makes effervescent'. ETHIOPIC: Te. fälḥa 'boil', Tna. fälḥe, Amh. fälla, Arg. fällaḥa, Har. fälaḥa, Gaf. fäla; also in Cushitic: Qem. bulä, Qua. bäl, Kham. bil.

From Ar. falak 'celestial sphere'; also in Amh. fäläk 'planet'. Geez ʾaflāk 'division, hypocrisy' is connected by Dillmann 1343 with Syr. plg (as probably derived from plg); however, it is rather to be derived from the verb falaka 'divide, create', a denominative of falak 'sky'. For the meaning 'divide, create', cp. fatara, below.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. tänfällälä, same.

meaning: 'split, divide, strike the first blow (in a combat), be the first in doing something (such as in giving a pledge or a deposit in order to keep someone from buying something)'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. flm, (i)f-ta-lama 'cut off the nose'. Ar. (Dat) falam 'notch, indent'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. fällämä 'begin', Te. fälma 'break to pieces', Amh. fälläma 'strike the first blow, initiate an action'.

phonetics: felmon ፌልሞን, filmon ፊልሞን, filmunte ፊልሙንተ, filmunji ፊልሙንጂ
meaning: 'such-and-such a place, a certain place, (T.M) a hidden place, an unidentified place, (K) so-and-so'
origin_explanation: from Gk. pheloumuni, phellani maenoni, transcription of Heb. pəlōni ʾalmōni, pəlōni. See also fälämənemunim, felälemlemonim. Löfgren 1927:136 explains the form with the final -te, -ti (as in filmunte) as a misreading of Gk. ei in pheloumunei as -ti.

meaning: 'kind of cloak, (K*) cover of a book, (T.M) box, trunk'
origin_explanation: from Gk. phailonē 'cloak'
from Gk. philippos 'horse lover'

SEMITIC: Ar. falaqa 'split, cleave', Sar. m-flq 'water distributed by opening a dam(?)', Sh. falaq 'split, crack', Aram. palaq, Syr. pelqā 'axe, hatchet', Md. pilqa, Akk. palaq/k/u 'slay, slaughter'. Akkadian pilaqqu is rendered 'axe' and according to Zimmern 12 it is borrowed from Sumerian, while von Soden 863 renders it 'stiletto, spindle' and does not consider it to be of Sumerian origin. For the origin of the root within the various Semitic languages, see Zimmern 12, Jeffery 229. For the origin of Gk. peleky s 'axe', see Masson 117; for additional bibliography on the Greek noun, see Jeffery 229, n. 2. On plq, plg, blq being phonetic variants, see Rabin 1980:21. ETHIOPIC: Tna. fälläqä 'gush out', Te. ('a)fläqa 'split', Amh. fälläqä, (tä)fläqälläqä 'gush out, spring from, sparkle, scintillate'. Arg. fälläqa, Gur. fälläqä, Har. fälläqa 'hit the head with a stone or a stick so that blood comes forth or the head swells'. The root is to be connected with flqq 'split': Tna. fälqäqä, Amh. fäläqäqä Gur. fäläqäqä.

meaning: 'behave unsuitably, straddle, be lustful, be impudent, be perverse, (M) be effeminate'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: for 'straddle', cp. Amh. (tä)fläqälläqä 'ride on an even-peed mule'.

meaning: 'storehouse, granary, (T) box, (Gr.488) reserve, stock, (Lt.K) vessel, heap'
origin_explanation: from Gk. phylakeion 'watchtower, fort'. See also Grébaut, Aethiopica 2 (1934).23-4.

meaning: 'depart, emigrate, go over to, be transferred, be removed, be banished, go into banishment, go into exile, be transformed, be changed, secede, split off, separate oneself, withdraw, sojourn abroad'
origin_explanation: aflasa 'make depart, send away, cast out, remove, banish, transfer, deport, shift, exile, transport, transplant, carry away as captive, change, separate'; tafalasa 'depart, be transferred'; tafālasa 'be transferred, wander from one place to another, emigrate, roam, pass from one generation to another, pass over to, an other, argue'; 'astafālasa 'remove, transfer'; falāsī (pl. falast) 'an exile, exiled, stranger, sojourner, one who is uprooted from a normal life, monk, ascetic, pilgrim, wanderer, proselyte'; falūs 'exiled, who is in exile, deported, migrant'; falās 'neighbor, sojourner'; falāsāwi 'stranger'; falāsiyāwi (M) 'pilgrim, stranger'; falsat 'departure, wandering, vagrancy, migration, travel, exile, transportation, assumption, death'; (som-a fälsat-ä 'Fast of the Assumption of the Holy Virgin [in the month of nahāse 1-15]', lit. 'the fast of her assumption'); maflos (maflas) 'wanderer, wanderer, savage, wild'; maflast, maflast 'path, by-path, backdoor'. SEMITIC: Sar. fls, 'put to flight', Heb. palles 'prepare a road' < 'break through' (see also [1935].151). PbH. pilles 'make a hole, pass through an end to end', Syr. palāš 'break through', palāšu 'break through, bore through'. ETHIOPIC: Te. fälsa 'emigrate, be driven away', Tna. fäläsa 'be uprooted (plant)', fälläsa 'become a monk' (denominative from fālast, from fls 'depart, separate oneself, withdraw'). Amh. fälläsa 'emigrate, be uprooted'. See also maflas.

from Ar. fulūs (pl. of fa/j/ls) 'a small coin', from Latin follis > Gk. phollis; also in Amh. falus 'money', Syr. pulšā 'a small copper coin'. For Ar. fulūs in various dialects, see Landberg 1942:2432ff.

falāsfā (T) 'be learned, philosophize' (denominative); tafālasfā 'philosophize, be a philosopher'; filāsof 'philosopher, (Gr) who likes philosophy'; filāsofāwi, filsofiwi (Gr.488) 'philosophical'; fälsāfe, fälsafännā 'philosophy'; tafālasfo (Gr.488) 'philosophy'; tafālasfoyāwi (Gr) 'philosophical'. from Gk. philosophos; also in Te. filosof 'philosopher'. Tna. fälasfa, Amh. fäläsfa, fäläsma.
from Gk. plastos 'fabricated, forged'; also in Syr. palastas, Aram. palastōn.

tafalsa (K), passive; tafālasa 'be split into small pieces, (T) glitter, shimmer'; fals (K*) 'broken piece'; falsat (Lt) 'headache'. SEMITIC: Ar. falasa 'escape from' (< 'separate, split'; for the development of meaning, see also fsl, psl (with metathesis): Ar. faṣala 'separate', Heb. piṣṣel 'bark, peel', Aram.-Syr. pṣal 'cleave, cut through', Md. pṣl; related to bṣl (see baṣala, above). ETHIOPIC: Te. fälläṣä 'split', Tna. fälläṣä, fälläṣä; see also falata. For fsl (with metathesis) > *fll, fṣl > fnčl (with augmented n), cp. Te. fänčäla 'separate'. Tna. fänčäla passed into Cushitic: Bil. fänčal 'split'. For 'headache', cp. Amh. falṣat.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. falṣut.
transcription of Heb. paletī, in hakkāreṭī wāhappaletī 'bodyguard of David'; also in Amh. felti 'king David's Philistine troops'.

meaning: 'separate, put asunder, disjoin, divide, split, segregate, sever, set apart, set aside, diversify, discern, make a distinction, distinguish, single out, determine, settle', 'cause. of falata; also 'make a distinction, speak clearly'
origin_explanation: tafalata, passive, reflexive; also 'be distinct, divorce'; tafālatä 'be separated from one another, depart from one another'; tafalafala, tafālafala 'be separated from one another'; maflāya (Gr.489) 'separation'. SEMITIC: Ar. falā (flw) 'wean', faliya 'be cut off', Sar. fly-t 'ordinance, regulation (?)', from fly 'separate, decide', Heb. pliy (hif-il) 'treat as special, distinguish between', Aram. palā (ply) 'split, cut open', Md. plā, Ug. ply 'be separated, be distinguished'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. fäläyä 'separate', Te. fäla (fly); also in Cushitic: Bil. fäley 'separate', Bil.qua. fal 'comb' (that is, 'separate the hair'), Sa. fil.
tafalya, passive; tafalaya, tafālawā 'be separated from one another'; maflāya (Gr.489) 'separation'.

meaning: 'oracular answer, oracle, (T.Sw. in D 1348) artist, artisan, craftsman'; (Gr.381) also, epithet of Christ;
origin_explanation: From Gk. phēmē 'prophetic saying'; also Christian-Ar. fīmī 'die Nennung der Titel und des Namens des zelebrierenden Bischofs oder Patriarchen vor der Epistel' (Graf 86). See also Worrell I:171, 177, Dobberahn 153.
from Coptic (Bohairic) phamenōt. See also baram-hāt.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. (a)fāndādādā 'bend, walk bent over', (tā)fonādādā 'be bent, be bowed'.
From Ar. funduq (of Greek [pandokheion] origin); also in Amh. fanduq.
ETHIOPIC: Te. fāndot 'buttock', Har. fuddi 'anus', Gur. fodo; also in Cushitic: Som. futto, Or. huddu; perhaps also Dar. fido 'genitals'. Cerulli 1936:246 derives Har. fuddi from Cushitic.

fand (pl. fanādāw) (K*) 'ripe cotton boll'. SEMITIC: related to Ar. fannada 'diversify'. ETHIOPIC: Te. fāndāwā 'divide into two parts'. Amh. fāndādā 'burst', Gur. fanāda; passed into Cushitic: Qab. finādā-yo.
fanfānt (K*), fanfāntūm (D.K*) 'hermaphro-dite'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. fanfānā 'abhor', Amh. fānfānā 'abhor, be a hermaphrodite', Tna. fānfāntī 'her-maphrodite', Amh. fānfānt, fanafānt, fanafāntī, fanfānt.

meaning: (T) = Amh. awē 'kind of animal credited with exhuming and eating corpses';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. fānfāfānā 'search for something (hen, dog)'.

meaning: (K*) 'decayed, spoiled, fetid' (Charles in Book of Jubilees 24:19 translates it 'valley');
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. (tā)fānnāgā 'stink, smell', fanga 'spoiled'.

meaning: (K*) 'go on a campaign on one's own';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. fānnānā.

meaning: (Lt) 'cut off the end of the nose';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. fonnānā.
from different case endings of Gk. phoiniks 'date palm'.

meaning: (K.T.Y) 'pry loose, dig up, tear up the ground (in search of things), examine, rummage through';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (Syria) fanqal 'overturn', probably fəqal 'reap crops' from the meaning 'pull out'. Brockelmann 1927:30 (following Dillmann 1371, Stade 25) compares Ar. faqara 'pierce, bore'. The Ethiopian form fanqala, fanaqqala being a 'Reimwortbildung' with naqala. ETHIOPIC: Te. fānqāla 'roll', Tna. fānqālā 'pry loose, displace', Amh. fānqāggālā, fānqaggālā, Gur. fən-qālā 'pull out something entrenched in the ground'.
from Gk. phoiniks, same.

meaning: 'long-tailed monkey which has black and white fur' (Amh. gureza); see Hom-mel 383. See also foqans.

origin_explanation: augmented by n in relation to faddan-rhea'.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. fanṭaṭa 'syphilis', Amh.Gur. fānṭaṭa 'smallpox'; on Amh. fanṭaṭa, see Strelcyn 1970 326ff. The root also occurs in Cushitic: fānṭaṭṭa, Bil.Sa. finṭaṭa, Or. fānṭaṭa, Som. fanṭo.

ETHIOPIC: Te. fānṭa (root fntw) 'separate, distin-guish, divide'. Tna. fānṭāwā, fānṭāwā, Amh. fānṭāwā, Gur. fānṭā 'divide in half'; Te.Tna.Am. fānṭāwā 'portion, share', Gur. fōnt 'half a loaf of bread'; passed into Cushitic: Bil. fānṭay 'separate', fānṭē.

meaning: 'send, send away, dismiss, send off, send forth, see off';
origin_explanation: tafannawa 'passive', tafannawā 'take leave of one another, bid fare-well to one another'; 'astafannawa 'accompany, bid farewell, see someone off, escort, send away'; fannāwī, act. part. of fannawa; fannawī, fem. fannūt 'sent'; fannā way, path, part, side, place, circum-stance, mode, concept'; (fannā sark 'towards evening, early evening'), (fannā nagh 'towards dawn'); (ba-fannā 'towards, in regard to'); fānot (pl. fannāw, fannāwāt) 'way, path, road, journey, course, wayside, side, street, passage, entrance, opportunity, mode'; (fānot-a 'towards, by way of'); fannāwē (K* Gr.490) 'sending (n.), missive', tafannāwī 'farewell'; SEMITIC: Ar. fanā (fnw) 'turn to, turn away', Soq. fənā 'go toward', Heb. pānā (pny) 'turn toward a direction', Aram.-Syr. pānā 'turn toward or upon', Md. pna, Ug. pn, Akk. panū. The meanings 'turn toward, go to' could have developed into the mean-ing 'make someone go to, turn toward', that is, 'send'. The above-mentioned verbs are denomina-tives from 'face, front': Ar. finā 'courtyard, open space in front of the house', SAr. fnw 'front (of building)', Mh. fenē 'before, in front of', Heb. pā-nīm 'face', Syr. pānītā 'side', Ug.Ph. pn 'face', Akk. pānū 'front'. For fannāw 'send' in relation to 'face' cp. Ar. (Hadr) wağğāh 'send' in connection with wağh 'face' (see Landberg 1942:2904). ETHIOPIC: Tna. fānnāwā 'release, abandon, let go, send', Te. fāna (fnw) 'depart', Amh. fna, fana 'direction, side (location)', fānot 'road' (from Geez), Gaf. fannā 'towards', Har. fōn, Gur. fōne 'go toward'.

meaning: 'cleave, split asunder, shatter, break through, split some-one's head, wound, injure';
origin_explanation: tafaqqa'a, passive; fəqā'at, fəqā'at (n.) 'cleaving, shattering, bruise, wound'. SEMITIC: Ar. faqqa'a 'explode'. related to faqa'a 'split', PBh. pāqa' 'split, burst asunder', Aram.-Syr. pāqa' (Syr. also parqa', with augmented r). Md. pqa. ETHIOPIC: Te. fāqqa, fāqqa'a 'split, hit on the head', Tna. fāqe'e, Amh. fāqqāqa 'make a cut or incision in the body', Gur. fāqqa 'split with an axe'; also in Cushitic: Kam. fiqqo, Bil. faq. See also faqq 1.

meaning: 'wish, desire, want, be willing, require, seek, request, need, be in need, permit, supervise, consider, regard, value, care for, take care of, look after, survey, review, muster, number, enumerate';
origin_explanation: afqaqda, causative; tafaqada 'be wanted, be desired, be required, be needed, be necessary, be numbered, be enumer-ated, be mustered'.
Cushitic: Som. ƒiqèn.

meaning: 'number, count, review, take account of, survey, muster, record, visit, seek out, inspect, supervise'
act. part. of ƒaqada; also 'who is missed, needy, inspector, wizard'
'wish, desire, will, intention, liking, volition, consent, pleasure, goodwill, permission'

'necessary, required, necessity (of life), need, wish, thing to be desired, thing that is desired, sought after, purpose, intention, business, petition, that which is missed, that which necessitates; goods, recompense, reward'
'place from which one keeps watch' (note that KG 1, 2 have wrongly maƒqaḍəyā)

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. ƒiqan 'container for water'; also in Cushitic: Som. ƒiqèn.
SEMITIC: Ar. ƒaqqa (ƒqq) 'split, separate' related to ƒaq'a. ETHIOPIC: Amh. ƒaƒqāqā 'scratch about'.

meaning: 'step aside, move over, get out of the way'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. ƒāqq bela, ƒāqāqq bela, bālā, Amh. ƒāqq alā, ƒōqāqq alā, ƒōqāqq bāyā; passed into Cushitic: Qem. ƒākāk 'be at a distance'. These meanings could derive from ƒaqq I.

meaning: 'love one another, make friends, be friendly, live in friendship'
'cause to be in love, cause to love one another'
'love, affection, friendship, amity, friendly friendliness, charity'
ƒaqār 'girdle, belt, cowl, vestment of a priest, (M) pantaloons with embroidered cuffs' (see Hammerschmidt 1970: 153)
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Syr. paqīrā, paqīlā 'headdress of a turban', from Gk. phakialion 'face cloth, turban' (Payne Smith 3224) < Lat. faciale 'face cloth, turban'. Ar. baqyār. ETHIOPIC: Amh. ƒiqar 'dress, pantaloons with embroidered cuffs' (from Geez).
ETHIOPIC: Tna. ƒera 'typhus, plague', Amh. ƒera Worrell III:133.

meaning: 'exult, spring, skip about, prance, dance, frolic'
'who exults, who dances, who frolics (Lt), same'

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (ta)bar'aṣa 'se remuer sous quelque chose (se dit, p. ex. d'un homme qu'on voit se remuer sous quelque chose qui le couvre serpent sous l'herbe)'; see Rūžička 231. The connection with Heb. par'oś 'flea' suggested by Dillmann 1354 (following Gesenius) is doubtful.

meaning: 'judge one another, be in litigation, (K*) be separated from one another'

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the basic meaning is 'separate, distinguish' > 'discern, judge' (for the development of meaning see Palache 59 under pll), Ar. ƒarada 'be separate, unique', aƒrada 'isolate', Sar. ƒrd-m 'uniquely, (that is, 'separately'), Heb. prd (niƒ'al) 'separated', Aram. pərad 'separate, scatter', S.rad 'place apart', Md. prd 'break through, apart'. ETHIOPIC: Te. ƒärda 'judge', Tna. ƒärädä, ƒärädä, Har. ƒärädä, Gur. ƒärädä; passed into Cushitic: Bil. ƒärād, Sa. ƒarad.

meaning: 'garment of a single layer of cloth'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. ƒarğ 'piece of cloth which is as wide as the width of the loom', or perhaps rather from ƒarada 'separate' (above), that is 'separated, single'.
would it be a name of a river?
ETHIOPIC: Tna. anƒarƒara 'writhe, flop around', Amh. tänƒarƒara; to be identified with ƒrƒr 'agitate' (see ƒarƒart).

SEMITIC: Ar. ƒarƒara 'cut', Heb. prr (hiƒ'il) 'break', Pbh. pirper 'crush, crumble, pulverize', prr (pi'el) 'crumble', Aram. parper, Ug. prr 'break', Akk. parāru. ETHIOPIC: Te. ƒarƒara 'be reduced to powder', Tna. ƒarƒara 'cut wood or a stone into small pieces, break into small pieces', ƒarƒar 'small pieces of wood', Amh. ƒarāƒarā 'crumble', Har. ƒirƒirāƒa, Gur. ƒa-rāƒarā; Arg. ƒərƒarī 'crumbs'; passed into Cushitic: Or. ƒirƒarī 'crumbs'. See also ƒrr, ƒarra, below.

SEMITIC: Ar. ƒurƒur 'small bird', Heb. parpar 'butterfly', from ƒrr, ƒrƒr, prpr 'flutter, quiver, move convulsively'. Ar. ƒarƒara 'move, shake wings', Soq. ƒrƒr 'fan', Heb. pōrar 'stir, rouse', Pbh. pirper, Aram.-Syr. parpar. ETHIOPIC: Te. ƒərƒarāt 'a bird', Amh. ƒarƒart 'partridge', Tna. ƒarƒar bālā 'palpitate', Har. ƒirƒir bāya 'be in convulsions'. See also ƒrƒr, anƒarƒara.
see ƒargaga, below.

meaning: 'heal (wound), become an ulcer, form (a scar), become inflamed'

origin_explanation: see also ƒargə'a.

meaning: 'make afraid, frighten, bring terror, terrify, make revere'; tafaraḥa 'be feared'; tafaraḥa (K*) 'fear one another'; 'astafaraḥa 'make fearful'
origin_explanation: farāḥī 'fearful, reverent'; farāḥ (KG) 'timorous, fearful, coward, timid'; fərūh 'feared, fearful, timid, careful'; fərāḥ 'fear, fright, terror, dread, awe, reverence'; tafərāḥī 'fright, fear, consternation'; mafraḥ 'fearful, terrifying, frightening, terrible, dreadful, fear-inspiring; fear, thing that excites fright'; mafraḥī 'fearful, terrifying, dreadful'. ETHIOPIC: Te. fārha 'fear, be afraid', Tna. fārhe, Amh.Arg. fārra, Har. fāra, Gur. fāre, Gaf. fāyā (for which see Leslau 1956:199).
SEMITIC: Ar. farḥ 'young bird', PbH. pāraḥ 'fly', Heb. 'epro'ḥ 'young bird', Aram.-Syr. parāḥ 'fly', Aram. parāḥ 'young bird', Syr. parāḥtā, Md. pra 'fly'.
SEMITIC: Ar. farḥa 'sprout', Heb. pāraḥ, Akk. parāḥu "'etwa gären'" (von Soden 827).
perhaps from Gk. perikallēs 'very pretty' (Müller).
perhaps w-fr (for which see safarā) with an enig-matic -lawa, for which see also sakā'lawa, ('as)fa'erlawa.

meaning: (T.D 1408) 'azure, (L) kind of tree of azure color'
Coptic month; misspelling for farmuti, below.

origin_explanation: from Coptic (Bohairic) pharmouthi. See also harmu-dā, marmudā.

meaning: 'enjoy oneself, take pleasure in, (T) be pampered'; farnus (K*) 'who enjoys himself'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. (tä)farnäsä 'lie down in relaxation', Amh. (tä)fārännäsä.

meaning: (T.Y.M) 'save, redeem, divide, separate, (K.GY) create'; 'afraqa (K*), causative; tafaraqa (K*), pass. of faraqa; farāqī 'savior, redeemer'; fərqān 'salvation, redemption';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: it is from the basic meaning 'split, detach' that the meanings 'free, save, deliver' developed: Ar. faraqa 'split, discriminate' (for the various mean-ings of Ar. furqān, see Jeffery 225ff), Sar. frq 'deliver, save', t-frq 'be dispersed, be scattered', Heb. pāraq 'tear off', Aram. pəraq 'separate', pāroqā 'save, redeem', pūrəqān 'deliverance, redemption', pəraq 'separate from', pāroqā 'savior', purqānā 'salvation', Md. prq 'sever, detach, free, deliver, Ug. prq 'break', Akk. parāqu 'separate'. ETHIOPIC: While it is possible that the meanings 'save, redeem' of G. faraqa derive 'divide, separate', as stated above for Semitic, it is also possible to consider the verb faraqa 'save, redeem' a denominative from fərqān 'salvation, re-demption'. As for G. fərqān, it is considered taken from Christian-Palestinian Aram. pūrəqān (see Jeffery 227). The root also exists in Te. fārqa 'pierce, perforate', Tna. fārāqä 'split, divide', Amh. fārāqä 'sepa-rate, divide', fārāqqäqa 'split', Gur. fārāqa 'split, tear off a branch'.
SEMITIC: Ar. frr, 'a-farra 'split', Heb. prr, (hif'il) 'break', Aram. pərar, Akk. parāru. ETHIOPIC: Tna. fārrärä 'dissolve', Amh. fār(r) 'furrow' (n.), from frr 'remove > shell, furrow'.

SEMITIC: Ar. faras 'horse', Sar. frs, Mh. Heb. päräs, Aram.-Syr. parāšā 'horseman', Md. päräsa. ETHIOPIC: Te. Tna.Amh.Gur. fārās 'horse', fārāz; common with Cushitic: Bil. fārda, fārāzā, Sa.Som. faras.

meaning: (T.Y.M) 'be demolished, be destroyed'; 'afrasā 'demolish, destroy'; fərsat 'destruction, demolition';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. farasa 'tear a prey', Hars. ferās 'break (bread)', PbH. pāras, päräs 'split up', Aram. paras 'divide, break up', Akk. 'separate', Brockelmann 1928:609 compares rata 'pierce', Aram.-Syr. parat 'split up', Md. prt. ETHIOPIC: Te. fārsa 'be ruined', Tna. Amh.Gur. fārräsä 'be demolished', Arg. (a) 'demolish'; passed into Cushitic: Be. ferās 'be stroyed', Bil. fārās, Qem. afārās 'uproot'.
of Persian (parasang, farsang) origin, through Parsahi; also Syr. parsāhā, Md. parsa. On this ure, see R. Pankhurst, JES 1969, 1:47, and 2369, col. 1.

meaning: (K*) 'make known, impose an 'afarsata (see farsāwe, below); farsāwī (K*) 'who imposes an 'afarsata'; farsāwe, farsut (K*) 'investigation in on a community to apprehend or ide wrongdoer';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. afarsata 'investigation imposed on a community', Tna. 'afarsata, 'afärseta.

SEMITIC: Ar. faraṣa 'cut', farada 'make an incision', Heb. pāraṣ, Aram. pāraṣ, Md. prṣ 'break through', Ug. prs 'open', Akk. parāṣu 'related to parāṣu 'separate', PbH. pāraṣ 'split', Aram. pəraṣ, Md. prt. ETHIOPIC: Tna. fārṭä 'bālā, fāṭärä 'bālā (with metathe-sis) 'be torn, burst', Amh. fārrätä 'burst, Har. fārṭäṭa, Har. fārṭäṭa, Gur. fārṭäṭā.
from Ar. farz; also in Amh. fārṣ, fārṭ, and probably Te. fərz.

meaning: 'bear fruit, produce fruit, yield fruit, be fruitful, (Lt) engender'; 'afrayā, causative; also 'bear fruit, produce fruit'; farāyī (K*), act. part. of farya; faray (K*) 'that bears fruit'; fəre (pl. fəreyāt, fərayāt) 'fruit, fruit (of land, womb, work, justice), blossom, bud, profit, offering'; fəryat 'bearing of fruit'; mafra'y, mafra'yī, 'that bears fruit, fruitful'; Qua. fər 'fruit', Bil. frī 'bear fruit'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. pərī 'fruit', Syr. pe(r)rā, Md. pira, Ph.Ug. pr. PbH. pərē, Heb. pärā (pry) 'bear fruit', Aram. prā, Syr. prā, pərī, Md. pra. For the related roots prr, prh, see Dietrich 302, under pra I (however, the quoted G. fari does not exist). ETHIOPIC: Te. fārya (fry) 'bear fruit', Tna. fāräyä, Amh.Gur. ('a)fārra; common with Cushitic: Kham. Qua. fər 'fruit', Bil. frī 'bear fruit'.
from Ar. farzā (Dozy) of Persian origin; also in Amh. fərz.

meaning: (T.T.S.K*) 'pamper, please, pro-duce fruit' (note that [Y] 'afārā 'be ashamed', the Amharic translation of fārzāzā, is to be corrected into 'afārra 'produce fruit' as given in Tayyä); tafarzaza 'burst, burst forth, crack open, leap up, burst out laughing, be scattered, be care-free and happy, be pampered, be pleased, (Gr.443) flower, blossom, open up (flower), (K*) be dispersed'; 'afarzaza (T.Y.M) 'form (scar having small pro-tuberances), form lumps on the body'; fərzūz (K*) 'who is carefree and happy, elated'; fərzāzē 'being carefree, being jovial, impetuosi-ty, elation, burst of laughter, being pampered (n.)';
origin_explanation: the basic meaning is probably 'burst, open up', hence 'produce' (whether fruit or scars) developing into 'burst out laughing, be happy, be pampered'. SEMITIC: Ar. fazza 'become excited with ardor', Heb. pzz (qal) 'be agile', (pi'el) 'leap', Syr. paz (pzz) 'leap'. In this case Ethiopic frzz is augmented by r. ETHIOPIC: Te. fārzäza 'burst forth, burst out laughing, be happy', Tna. fārzūz 'joyful', Amh.

meaning: be happy, be without worries, be carefree, be pampered, produce much fruit'. Gur. f°änfäze bäla 'feel relaxed'. Geez (tä) fänfäza 'is not to be connected with Te. fäzza (fäzz) 'to have one's eyes open', as suggested by Littmann-Höfner 672.

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. fäsa (fäsy), Tna. fäsäwä, Amh.Arg. fäsa, Gaf. f°äsa, Gur. fäsä, Har. fäs äsa; passed into Cushitic: Bil. fäsa.

meaning: 'outside the limits, protruding (eyes), bulging'. see also fäsus, below.
(KG) 'bad odor due to indigestion, bad smell, belch'.
'Easter, Passover, paschal lamb'; (sanbata mahätäwa fäsikä 'Easter eve');
origin_explanation: from Gk. paskha, phasek, of Hebrew (pesah) origin; also in Tna. fäsika, fäsiga, Te. fäziga, Amh. fäsika, fäziga. See also fäšh, päšä, päšikä.

meaning: (M.Gr.383) 'draw lots, play a game of chance by using fingers'; täfäsasa 'draw lots mutually, use fingers in a guessing game'; fäss (K*) 'lot';
origin_explanation: from Syr. pessä 'lot'; also Aram. payis 'arbitration, allotment', paysä 'lot', ballotting', passä 'lot' (Fraenkel 60, 289, Noeldeke 1910:45 [correct täfäsaƒa into täfäsasa]). For the description of allotting things by using fingers, cp. also PbH. payis 'arbitration, esp. by counting out a certain number on the raised fingers of those among whom a decision is to be made' (Jastrow 1162). ETHIOPIC: perhaps Te. fäsäs, fäsäsa '(military) billetting, provisioning' (if it is done by drawing lots), Tna. fäsäs 'tax, tribute', Amh. fäsäs 'tax in food or grain for soldiers, which is imposed on a district'.
SEMITIC: Ar. fäsa (fsw) 'break wind'.

meaning: 'rejoice, be glad, be cheerful, be merry, enjoy oneself, be comforted'; (täfäššah 'hail!'); astäfäššaha 'gladden, cause to rejoice, delight, comfort, make one enjoy himself, make joyous, make glad'; fäššuh 'glad, joyful, jubilant, delightful, pleasant, merry, happy, joyous, rejoicing, cheerful'; fäššäha 'joy, gladness, happiness, mirth, comfort, consolation'; täfäššht 'joy, gladness, enjoyment, delight'; täfäššähäyat (Gr) 'wishes of joy'; mästäfäššah 'who bestows gladness, rejoicer, pleasant, delightful'; mästäfäššahi, (Gr) mästäfäššahi, same.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. fäšuha 'be wide, be spacious', 'infasuha 'be dilated with joy' (Dillmann 1348), fäših 'free from distress of mind', Ar. (Syria) t-fäššah 'rejoice', Sär. h-fš.h 'cause to rejoice', Akk. pašahu 'become soothed, become appeased'. Vollers. ZA 9 (1894) compares Heb. päsah, originally 'dance', and Ar. fäšäha 'part the legs' (see also Landberg 1920:355). For Heb. päso°h in Is. 31:5, meaning 'Raum schaffen', see Kopf 166. The connection with Heb. säme°h 'be joyful' suggested by Barth 1893:33 is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Te. fäsäsäh 'bela 'rejoice'', Tna. fäšhäyä, (tä)fäššähe, Amh. fäšša 'be joyful', (from Geez); passed into Cushitic: Sa. fäšuh 'joyful'.

meaning: 'cut, break with a hammer, split with a wedge, cut stones, hew out'; täfäšha, passive; fäšuh, pass. part. of fäšha; mäfšah, mäfšäht (pl. mäfäšäht) 'hammer, axe, wedge, blow of a chisel, stonecutter';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. fädaha 'break, crush', Ar. (Syria) 'wound on the head'; also fahasa (with metathesis) 'dig, hollow out', Heb. pšh (pšl) 'break to pieces'. Neo-Assyrian pašahu; related to pss (Heb. pšš) and psy. Isy: Ar. fäšä (fšy), Heb. päsä (pšy) 'set the mouth'. Aram. pašä, Syr. pašši (psy) 'set'. Md. psa. ETHIOPIC: Tna. fäššähe 'break to pieces', Te. fäššäwa 'break' (intr.), fänšäh bäla 'split to pieces' (inserted n); related to fsy in Amh. fäčča 'grind flour', Arg. fäčča, Har. fäča, Gur. fäča; in reduplicative form in Tna. fäšfäša, fäčfäča 'cut into pieces', Te. fäčfäča 'smash, crush'; passed into Cushitic: Som. fafačči (from fčfč) 'grind coarsely'.

meaning: 'fill, complete, accomplish, finish, consume, end, fulfill, cute, carry out, satisfy, perfect, perform, exhaust, stop'; afassama, causative; also 'order the completion, render perfect'; täfassama, passive; also 'die right away, be effected, (T) worship idols, be consecrated'; astäfassama (Lt) 'bring to an end, accomplish'; fassämi, act. participle; fassum 'accomplished, completed, finished, filled, filled full, complete, perfect, perfect, whole, unblemished'; (fassuma 'completely, perfectly, entirely, fully, through and through'; negative verb + fassuma 'not at all'); (fassumän 'ahaw 'blood brothers', lit. 'full brothers'); fasm 'end, consummation, termination'; fassäme 'completion, conclusion, execution, end, consummation, perfection, fullness, consecration'; fassämmä 'perfection, integrity'; mäfassum 'who fulfills, who accomplishes, who executes, who completes, who perfects'; mäfassäm (Lt) 'end';
origin_explanation: tafšäm 'completion, ending'; tafšämi 'last, that brings to completion'; tafšämet 'completion, fulfillment, consummation, end, performance, perfection'. SEMITIC: Ar. fäšama 'cease, pass away' could be derived either from the meaning 'break' (Ar. fasama, Heb. päsäm), or from 'be finished, come to an end' (G. fassama), that is, 'cease'. ETHIOPIC: Te. fäšma 'finish', Tna. fässämä, Amh. fässämä, fättämä, Arg. fättämä, Gaf. fittämä, Gur. fässämä. See also fädama.

meaning: 'front, forehead, forefront (e.g. of temple), avant-garde'; fäsma 'before, in front (of), in advance, foremost, in opposition (to)'; fäsum 'who is in front line, predecessor, forerunner';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Gur. fänčä, finčä 'forehead' < *fämčä < *fäsmä (with metathesis) < *fäsmä. The connection between Gur. fänčä and Amh. bä-fänta 'instead of' suggested by Rundgren 1955:86 is doubtful.

meaning: (T.TS) 'whistle, hiss, rustle'; täfäšaya 'whistle, hiss, (L) rejoice exceedingly'; astäfäšawa 'whistle'; fäšä, fäs 'whistle, whistling, hissing, rustle';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. sipsep 'chirp, whisper' (with Dillmann 1390, against Cerulli 1936:246 who derives the root from Cushitic); also Ar. safsafa, PbH. sipsep 'whis-tle'. Brockelmann 1928:443 compares also Syr. nasap 'hiss'. Aram. näsep 'chirp'; onomatopoetic. ETHIOPIC: Te. fäsa (fsy) 'whistle', Tna. fäsäyä, fäs abälä, Amh. (a)f°äččä, (an)f°äččä, Arg. (a)f°äčča, Har. (a)feča, fič äsa; also in Cushitic: Kam.Al. fiqqo 'whistle', Qab. fiqqo (for q.č; see Cohen 1931:397). Som. fodi 'play the flute'.
SEMITIC: Ar. fatfata 'crumble' (Dozy), PbH. pitpet, Syr. patpet; also Ar. fatta 'crumble', Heb. pätal. Syr. pat.

meaning: 'open, loosen, let loose, untie, unfasten, release, dissolve, disengage, make of no effect, set free, solve, absolve, forgive (sins), give absolution, break a fast, do justice, judge, pass judgment, administer justice, arbitrate, decide, interpret'; (fətḥa läwəlä 'condemn'); 'afətḥa, causative; also 'bring to judgment, ask for justice, release, set free'; təfətḥa, passive; also 'relax'; təfətḥaṭəḥa 'be separated from one another, bring suit, sue one another, engage in a legal case, be in court with someone, stand trial, condemn, contend'; 'astafətḥaṭəḥa, causative of təfətḥaṭəḥa; fətḥi, act. part. of fətḥa; also 'judge'; fətuḥ, pass. part. of fətḥa; also 'open, unconstrained, discharged, absolved'; fətḥ 'precept, law, suit, lawsuit, justice, judgment, proceeding, decision, verdict, sentence (in court)'; (fətḥa nagaśt 'Code of the Kings', title of a book; for a bibliography, see Hammer-Schmidt 1973:174); (fətḥa mot 'death sentence'); (manbara fətḥ 'tribunal, court of justice'); ('awda fətḥ, same); fətḥat 'opening (n), absolution, judging (n), provision'; fətḥatəḥo (T) 'lawsuit, litigation'; mafətḥ 'Gr.490) 'motive for a decision'; mafətḥ 'solution, solving, (M) instrument for opening (key)'; mafətḥi, (M) mafətḥi 'opener, who dissolves, who sets free'; (mafətḥe śəräy 'prayer against charms', lit. 'that loosens the charms');
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. fatḥa 'open', fatḥ 'judgment, decision' (in the Qur'an, from Geez; see Jeffery 221, contrary to Müller who considers it Arabic in view of SAr. ftḥ), SAr. ḥ-ftḥ 'obtain a judicial order', ftḥ 'lawsuit, judicial order', Šh. fətaḥ 'open', Heb. pätaḥ, Aram.-Syr. pətaḥ, Md.Ph.Ug. ptḥ, Akk. petū, patū.

meaning: 'fortified places' a variant reading of fəšəḥatāt (see fəšḥ II).

'spin, twist'; təfatala, passive; fətli, act. part. of fatala; fəțul 'twisted, spun'; fətəl (pl. 'afətəl, 'afətəlt) 'thread, cord, wick, string'; (fətla śarət 'spider web'); fəṭəl (Lt) 'toga'; fətəlo 'network, thread'; fəṭlat (n.) 'spinning, twisting';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. fatala 'twist, spin', Šh. fəṭəl 'roll a wick', Heb. päṭal 'twist', Aram.-Syr. pəṭal, Md. pṭil, Akk. pəṭālu 'spin'. ETHIOPIC: Te. fätla 'twist, weave', Tna. fätäla, Amh.Gaf.Gur. fäṭäla 'spin, twist, braid', Arg. fätäla, Har. fätäla; passed into Cushitic: Sa.Af. fätal 'spin'.

meaning: 'try, test, put to test, investigate, scrutinize, explore, examine, search, probe, tempt, prove, probe, (T) melt'; 'afṭana 'allow to be explored or examined, confess'; təfatana, passive of fatana; təfatana, reciprocal of fatana; 'astafətana (K*), causative of təfatana; fəṭani 'examiner, explorer, investigator'; fəṭun 'tested, tried, investigated, examined, explored'; fəṭana 'proof, experiment, trial, attempt, temptation'; fəṭanat (n) 'testing, probing, trial'; mafṭani, participle of 'afṭana; also 'means of testing';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. fatana 'tempt, seduce', Mh. fəṭən, Syr. fəṭən 'tumult, discord', Md. piṭna 'temptation' (from Arabic). ETHIOPIC: Te. fäṭna 'attempt, prove, seduce', 'afṭäna 'try', Tna.Amh. fäṭṭäna 'test, try, examine', Arg. fəṭṭäna, Gur. fəṭäna; passed into Cushitic: Sa.Af. fäṭan 'test', Bil. fäṭan.

meaning: (M) 'hurry, cause to be fast' is a misprint for 'afṭana.

'break off a piece, fracture, crush, break the Host during the communion, break and distribute (bread or anything else), give out, make a gift, appoint a portion, give a share'; fəṭṭat(t)ata, passive; fəṭṭati (K*), act. part. of fəṭṭata; fəṭṭat 'broken, fracture, fragment'; fəṭṭat 'piece, fragment, scrap, morsel, piece of bread'; fəṭṭatə 'breaking the holy bread for distribution'; fəṭṭato 'fraction, portion'; (salota fəṭṭato 'prayer when breaking the bread for the Eucharist'); fəṭṭ, fəṭṭa, (T.M) fəṭṭo 'piece, portion, fraction, morsel, gift';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. fatta (fṭṭ) 'break', Soq. en-fetiṭ 'break in small pieces', Heb. päṭat, PbH. päṭat, Aram. pəṭat, Syr. paṭ, paṭpeṭ 'crumble', (with augmented r) 'crumbs', Md. piṭṭ 'crumble'. ETHIOPIC: Te. fäṭaṭa, fäṭṭaṭa, fäṭṭaṭa 'crumble bread, break into small pieces', Tna. fäṭäṭa, fäṭṭäṭa, Amh. fäṭäṭa, fäṭäfäṭa, Gur. fäṭäṭa; passed into Cushitic: Bil. färtäṭ 'crumble', Sa. fäṭat, Be. fəṭiṭ 'divide'. See also fatfata.

meaning: 'desire, wish, love, covet, lust for, have a liking for'; 'afṭawa, causative; also (with object suffix pronouns) 'please, satisfy'; təfatwa, passive; also 'long for, be desirable'; fəṭawi, act. part. of fatwa; also 'lustful, covetous'; fəṭaw 'desired, desirable, pleasing, pleasant, delightful'; fəṭwat, fəṭot 'desire, wish, desirable thing, concupiscence, craving, liking (for), being desired, pleasure'; fəṭaw, same;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. fatāwī 'lust, given to desire'; mafṭaw 'lust, wish, (it is) fitting, suitable, fit, becoming, convenient, seemly, proper, (it is) necessary, obligatory, that gives pleasure, pleasant, pleasing, delightful, precious, desirable, desired; (Gr) enticement'. ETHIOPIC: Te. fäṭa (fṭw) 'love, wish, desire', Tna. fäṭäwa, Amh. fäṭwat 'desire, lust' (from Geez); also Amh. ('a)fäṭäṭa 'wish, like' (a secondary form); passed into Cushitic: Bil. fäṭay 'friend'.

meaning: 'be fast, be swift, hurry, be in a hurry, be prompt, speed up'; 'afṭana 'make haste, hasten, accelerate, hurry, move swiftly, (with an infinitive) hurry, do quickly, do hastily'; təfafṭana 'press, urge, succeed in doing fast'; 'astafafṭana 'quicken, make haste, hasten, accelerate, urge, overtake'; fəṭṭan (Lt) 'swift, rapid'; fəṭun 'swift, quick, rapid, prompt, hastening'; (ba-fəṭun, fəṭuna 'immediately, right away, promptly, speedily, quickly, swiftly, suddenly'); fəṭnat (fem. fəṭṭan) 'swift, hasty, sudden'; fəṭnat 'rapidity, velocity'; mafṭani, (Gr) 'afṭani 'who accelerates, hasty, hurried, rapid, sudden'; mastafəṭani, mastafəṭani, (K*) mastafəṭən, same;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. fatuna 'be clever, discerning'. For the relation between 'clever, discerning' and 'fast', cp. Heb. maher 'fast', mäḥir 'skilled' and Geez (ta)mohra 'study'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. fäṭäna 'be rapid', Amh. fäṭṭäna, Har. fäṭna, Gur. fäṭänä; Te. fäṭna (and also Bil. fäṭan) 'understand' is borrowed from Arabic.

meaning: 'create, fashion, produce, fabricate, cause, devise, invent, feign, contrive, inscribe (or carve) magic letters, make incisions on the flesh'; təfatəra, passive;
'invention, fiction, falsehood, designs, incisions, cuttings, magical figures, (T*) idol'
'act of creating, creation, things created, creature, nature, character, disposition, constitution, origin, kind, species, generation, family'
'place where things are done, origin, provenance'
'dark wood, (T.K) rotten log or wood, name of a tree'
origin_explanation: "Wenn es sich um eine 'dark root' (statt 'wood') und um einen 'name of plant' (statt 'tree') handeln würde, könnte man an arabisch fuwwa 'Krapp, Färberröte, Rubia tinctorum L.' denken, der in Süd-arabien der roten Wurzel wegen angebaut wird" (Müller).
ETHIOPIC: Amh. fayd, same.
ETHIOPIC: perhaps Tna. fah bālā 'be spread, be dispersed'. See also fw', fw'.
from Ar. fa'l 'omen'; also in Amh. Gur. fal, Har. fāl; in Cushitic: Or. fala, Bil. fāl. On Ar. fa'l in the various Arabic dialects, see Landberg 1942:2391 ff. See also wfl.

meaning: 'be restless, be fidgety, start (someone who is surprised)'
'jump up, spring up, go up, boil over'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: perhaps in connection with Ar. fāqa (fwq) 'surpass', fawqu 'up, upstairs, on top'.

meaning: 'recover from illness, be cured, be healed, take care of oneself, be appeased, be smoothed'
'healing, cure, medecine, remedy, (Lt.K*) poison, medicinal herb'

phonetics: (faws manfasāwi 'Spiritual Healing', title of a book; for a bibliography, see Hammer-Schmidt-Six 1983:225)

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 1376 (followed by Barth 1893: 14) compares Ar. šafā (šfy) 'heal'. The connection with Syr. pāš (pwš) 'remain, expect, stay behind' suggested by Brockelmann 1928:561 is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Tna. fawwāšā 'heal, cure', Amh. fāwwāšā (from Geez).
ETHIOPIC: Te. fāwīta 'dissolute woman', fāyṭāt 'prostitute', Tna. fāṭāṭā 'be given to a dissolute life'.

meaning: 'wipe off, wipe clean, efface, rub (the back), clean by rubbing, dry by wiping, (T.Y) sweep'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. fawāzā 'scrape'.
from Gk. phialē 'bowl, drinking bowl'; also piyalē (see Krauss 443), Md. piala.
ETHIOPIC: related to Te. fānna (fnn) 'explain, interpret'.
ETHIOPIC: perhaps Te. feṭāṭa 'knock down', Amh. fāyāṭā 'rob', fāyāṭawi 'thief' (from Geez; only with reference to the thieves crucified with Christ).
from Amh. fāyyāṭā, denominative from fī 'face'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. (tā)'af'af'aṭā (from foṭā) 'dash about (several persons, animals)'. See also fṭṭ.
ETHIOPIC: Amh fazuq (probably from Geez); perhaps also Te. fazuq 'place of execution' (which may have some connection with a spear).
ETHIOPIC: Te. fāzazā (fzz) 'have one's eyes opened', Tna. fāzāzā 'be stupefied', Amh. fāzzāzā 'be in a stupor, be dazed'.
